title: CatLLM: A Reproducible LLM Pipeline for Classifying Open-Ended Text, Images, and PDFs Across Domains
description:
CatLLM is an ecosystem of Python packages that use large language and vision models to automate the categorization of open-ended text, images, and PDFs across research domains. It produces consistent, highly reproducible output optimized for statistical analysis (CSV export, ensemble voting, agreement scores, batch processing).

The cat-llm package is a meta-package that installs the full family of domain-specific classification packages. Each sub-package can also be installed individually for a lighter footprint.

Sub-packages bundled in this release:

  • cat-stack — general-purpose base library and shared infrastructure (UnifiedLLMClient, provider configs, ensemble voting, JSON extraction, embeddings, formatter fallback). All other packages depend on this.
  • cat-survey — survey response classification
  • cat-vader — social media text classification
  • cat-ademic — academic paper classification
  • cat-pol — political text classification
  • cat-cog — cognitive assessment scoring (CERAD constructional praxis, etc.)
  • cat-web — web content classification

Every domain package exposes the same five core functions — classify(), extract(), explore(), summarize(), prompt_tune() — with domain-specific parameters layered on top.

Capabilities:

  • Text — multi-label classification, category extraction, saturation analysis, ensemble voting across models
  • Images — feature classification, open-ended identification, quality scoring
  • PDFs — page-level classification (text, image, or both modes), structured extraction
  • Summarization — paragraph, bullet, one-liner, structured, and full-report modes for text and PDFs
  • Prompt optimization — automatic prompt tuning from user feedback

Supported providers: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Mistral, Perplexity, xAI, HuggingFace, Ollama (local), DeepSeek.

With leading models (GPT-5, Gemini, Qwen 3), CatLLM achieves 98% accuracy compared to human consensus on classification tasks.

R and Stata wrapper packages are also available — see the GitHub repository.
